summary: Tractate Yoma daf 77a covers: Bathing prohibition derived from verse; Ezekiel's anointing vision; what counts as bathing; Whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (affliction); Prohibition on anointing; Na'aman's anointing; pleasure vs. necessity; Sandals: prohibition on wearing sandals on Yom Kippur.

display.whats: Bathing prohibition derived from verse; Ezekiel's anointing vision; what counts as bathing
display.hint: The Gemara derives the bathing prohibition on Yom Kippur from a verse, and connects it to Ezekiel's vision where anointing with oil is forbidden. The discussion explores what actions qualify as bathin...

learning.summary: The Gemara derives the bathing prohibition on Yom Kippur from a verse, and connects it to Ezekiel's vision where anointing with oil is forbidden. The discussion explores what actions qualify as bathing for purposes of the prohibition.
learning.ahaMoment: The bathing prohibition is derived from the same scriptural tradition as the YK service - the whole fast has a textual foundation, not just custom.
learning.memoryAnchor: Bathing prohibition: derived from text, not tradition alone - the body's purity joins its affliction.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about bathing prohibition derived from verse; ezekiel's anointing ?

argumentFlow.label: Ezekiel's vision of sun-worshippers
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Ezekiel sees twenty-five men between the porch and the altar with their backs to the Sanctuary, worshipping the sun toward the east. The Gemara reads 'their backs toward the Temple' as teaching that they openly degraded the Divine Presence, the verse using a euphemism for their conduct.

argumentFlow.label: Michael pleads, Gabriel is sent to scatter the coals
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: God tells the angel Michael that his nation has sinned; Michael asks that the good be spared, but God responds that He will burn even the good among them, since they failed to rebuke the wicked. God then orders Gabriel to take coals of fire from between the cherubs and scatter them over the city.

argumentFlow.label: Why the coals cooled in transit
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Chana bar Bizna in the name of Rabbi Shimon Chasida teaches that had the coals not cooled as they passed from the cherub's hand to Gabriel's hand, no remnant of the Jewish people would have survived. The delay in handing over the coals tempered the severity of the punishment.

theological: The bathing prohibition is derived from the same scriptural tradition as the YK
narrative: Bathing prohibition derived from verse; Ezekiel's anointing

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of bathing prohibition context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume the angelic narrative is a digression with no halachic bearing, just aggada about Ezekiel's vision.
misconceptions.correction: The passage is woven into the larger sugya on Yom Kippur afflictions and divine judgment, and the Gemara uses these stories about Gabriel, the coals, and the wives of Torah scholars to make pointed claims about reward, restraint in punishment, and merit.

visualizableElements.name: Bathing prohibition derived from verse; Ezekiel's

quizSeeds.q: Why does the Gemara stress that the coals cooled as they passed from the cherub to Gabriel?
quizSeeds.a: The cooling shows that the punishment was deliberately tempered. Had Gabriel taken the still-hot coals directly, the destruction would have been total; the delay in the handoff is what left a remnant of the Jewish people alive.

quizSeeds.q: Why was Gabriel punished even though he carried out the mission he was sent on?
quizSeeds.a: Not for failing, but for reporting back in full detail. The principle is that an agent of destruction should only hint at the outcome, not announce its success, so Gabriel was struck for delivering an open report of the devastation.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara derives the bathing prohibition on Yom Kippur from a verse, and connects it to Ezekiel's vision where anointing with oil is forbidden. The

display.whats: Whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (affliction)
display.hint: The Gemara asks whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (self-affliction) as required by the Torah.

learning.summary: The Gemara asks whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (self-affliction) as required by the Torah.
learning.ahaMoment: Refraining from bathing is inui precisely because bathing is a pleasure; removing pleasures is the mechanism of YK affliction.
learning.memoryAnchor: Is not-bathing an affliction? Only because bathing is normally a pleasure.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (affliction?

argumentFlow.label: Trying to prove bathing is affliction from David's flight
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara seeks a source that abstaining from bathing counts as affliction. It cites that David's men were 'hungry, weary, and thirsty': hunger from lack of bread, thirst from lack of water, and 'weary' apparently from lack of bathing. The Gemara objects that 'weary' might instead mean lack of wearing shoes, so the proof fails.

argumentFlow.label: Deriving bathing as affliction from 'on a weary soul'
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yitzchak offers a better source: 'As cold water on a weary soul.' The Gemara considers that 'weary' might mean thirst, but rejects this: the verse says cold water 'on' a weary soul, not 'in' it. Water placed on the body, rather than drunk, points to bathing, establishing that withholding it is affliction.

theological: Refraining from bathing is inui precisely because bathing is a pleasure; removin
narrative: Whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (affliction

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners think the Gemara is debating whether bathing is pleasurable, treating the question as common sense.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara already assumes bathing is a comfort; what it is actually hunting for is a scriptural verse that the word 'inui' (affliction) encompasses going unwashed, which is why it parses prepositions like 'on' versus 'in' so closely.

visualizableElements.name: Whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (

quizSeeds.q: Why does the first attempt to prove that withholding bathing is affliction fail?
quizSeeds.a: The verse calls David's men 'weary,' but the Gemara cannot show that 'weary' specifically means unwashed; it could just as well refer to being without shoes, so the word does not uniquely establish bathing as the missing comfort.

quizSeeds.q: How does the wording 'cold water on a weary soul' settle that bathing is the relevant affliction?
quizSeeds.a: The preposition 'on' rather than 'in' is decisive. Water drunk would enter the soul; water 'on' the soul is applied externally, which is bathing, so the verse ties weariness to the lack of washing rather than thirst.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara asks whether refraining from bathing constitutes inui (self-affliction) as required by the Torah.

display.whats: Prohibition on anointing; Na'aman's anointing; pleasure vs. necessity
display.hint: The prohibition on anointing applies to pleasure anointing; the story of Na'aman illustrates the distinction between permitted and prohibited anointing.

learning.summary: The prohibition on anointing applies to pleasure anointing; the story of Na'aman illustrates the distinction between permitted and prohibited anointing.
learning.ahaMoment: Na'aman anointed for health, not pleasure - the distinction matters: only pleasure-anointing is forbidden on YK.
learning.memoryAnchor: Na'aman anointed for health - pleasure anointing is the forbidden kind.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about prohibition on anointing; na'aman's anointing; pleasure vs. ?

argumentFlow.label: Trying to prove going barefoot is affliction from David
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara asks for the source that not wearing shoes is an affliction, citing that David went up the Mount of Olives weeping, head covered, and barefoot. The proof is rejected: 'barefoot' might describe a king deprived of his horse and whip rather than literally lacking shoes.

argumentFlow.label: Isaiah's barefoot walk and the patched-shoes challenge
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak cites Isaiah, commanded to remove his shoe and who then walked 'naked and barefoot.' The Gemara objects that 'barefoot' might mean patched shoes, just as 'naked' must mean ragged garments rather than literally unclothed, so this verse also fails to prove the point.

argumentFlow.label: Deriving the shoe affliction from Jeremiah
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak gives the conclusive source from Jeremiah: 'Withhold your foot from being barefoot,' read homiletically as keeping oneself from sin so the foot does not come to be bare. The verse treats being barefoot as a hardship to be avoided, establishing that going without shoes is an affliction.

theological: Na'aman anointed for health, not pleasure - the distinction matters: only pleasu
narrative: Prohibition on anointing; Na'aman's anointing; pleasure vs.

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of anointing prohibition context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners expect the first verse showing someone barefoot to settle that going shoeless is an affliction.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara repeatedly rejects verses where 'barefoot' can be read non-literally (a king without his horse, patched shoes) and accepts only Jeremiah's verse, where being barefoot is plainly cast as a hardship to be withheld from.

visualizableElements.name: Prohibition on anointing; Na'aman's anointing; ple

quizSeeds.q: Why does the verse about David walking barefoot up the Mount of Olives fail as a source for the shoe affliction?
quizSeeds.a: Because a king's 'barefoot' state could be metaphorical: a king stripped of his horse and whip was described as going barefoot even while wearing shoes, so the verse does not prove that literally lacking shoes is an affliction.

quizSeeds.q: How does the rejection of Isaiah's 'naked and barefoot' parallel the patched-shoes reading?
quizSeeds.a: Just as 'naked' cannot be taken literally and must mean ragged clothing, 'barefoot' can be softened to mean patched shoes. The two terms are read consistently, so the verse loses its force as a literal proof about shoelessness.

sugyot.finalRuling: The prohibition on anointing applies to pleasure anointing; the story of Na'aman illustrates the distinction between permitted and prohibited anointin

display.whats: Sandals: prohibition on wearing sandals on Yom Kippur
display.hint: The Mishna rules that wearing sandals is prohibited on Yom Kippur; this is one of the five afflictions.

learning.summary: The Mishna rules that wearing sandals is prohibited on Yom Kippur; this is one of the five afflictions.
learning.ahaMoment: Even sandal leather signals status and comfort - removing it strips the wearer of ordinary bodily dignity as an act of repentance.
learning.memoryAnchor: No sandals on YK: even footwear is a comfort, and comfort is set aside.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about sandals: prohibition on wearing sandals on yom kippur?

argumentFlow.label: Deriving that withholding marital relations is affliction
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara turns to another Yom Kippur affliction and asks where the Torah calls refraining from conjugal relations 'inui.' It answers from Laban's words to Jacob, 'If you shall afflict my daughters,' which the Gemara understands as a warning against withholding marital relations.

theological: Even sandal leather signals status and comfort - removing it strips the wearer o
narrative: Sandals: prohibition on wearing sandals on Yom Kippur

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners may think the Yom Kippur afflictions are an open list and that marital abstention is simply one obvious example.
misconceptions.correction: Each affliction needs a textual anchor. The Gemara specifically grounds marital abstention as 'inui' in Laban's words to Jacob, treating the derivation as necessary rather than self-evident.

visualizableElements.name: Sandals: prohibition on wearing sandals on Yom Kip

quizSeeds.q: From which verse does the Gemara learn that withholding marital relations counts as 'inui'?
quizSeeds.a: From Laban's charge to Jacob, 'If you shall afflict my daughters, and if you shall take other wives,' where 'afflict' is read as withholding conjugal relations from Leah and Rachel.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Mishna rules that wearing sandals is prohibited on Yom Kippur; this is one of the five afflictions.

rashiTranslations.he: לבוש הבדים. הוא גבריאל בספר דניאל: [פולסא דנורא. מקלות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi on the bathing prohibition: explains the derivation from the verse.

rashiTranslations.he: של אור: אי לא עבדת. כלל: לא עבדת. והיינו מצדיקים אותך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the Ezekiel passage referenced is about the Babylonian exile.

rashiTranslations.he: לומר דוחה הוא את הדבר אולי ישוב הקב"ה מחרונו ויתרצה להם:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: defines what constitutes bathing for purposes of the prohibition.

rashiTranslations.he: כדפקדוך. שתהא אתה נוטל הגחלים מבינות לכרובים ואתה אמרת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: distinguishes between full bathing and partial washing.
Rashi: explains why bathing is classified as pleasure rather than necessity.
Rashi: the source verse for bathing prohibition on Yom Kippur.
Rashi: explains the term used for affliction in the relevant verse.
Rashi: connects the bathing prohibition to the broader five afflictions.

rashiTranslations.he: בלשון פרסי לפי שהוא מעלה שם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the inui discussion - whether withholding bathing is itself an affliction.
Rashi: the definition of inui as applied to bathing.
Rashi: Na'aman's case - anointing for health rather than pleasure.
Rashi: distinguishes medicinal anointing from pleasure anointing.
Rashi: the anointing prohibition on Yom Kippur applies to pleasure only.
Rashi: sandal prohibition - leather shoes that signify status.
Rashi: explains what qualifies as a sandal under the prohibition.

rashiTranslations.he: עינוי וכתיב כי אמרו העם רעב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.
Rashi glosses the verse 'and faint and thirsty in the wilderness' (2 Sam 17): it refers to wanting cold water, showing that lack of washing is what counts as fatigue.
Continuing: 'upon a faint soul' (Prov 25) - so it is washing whose absence is called being faint, not the lack of wearing sandals; and yet Scripture still calls that an affliction.

rashiTranslations.he: מיקרי עיף ולא מנעילת הסנדל וקרי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: this proves that going without washing is what is termed 'faint,' not going without sandals - and Scripture nonetheless calls the latter an affliction.

rashiTranslations.he: ליה עינוי דכתיב בכל אשר התענה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: as it is written 'in all that he afflicted himself' (1 Kings 2), so forgoing the sandal is also counted as a self-affliction.

rashiTranslations.he: אבי: מרטקא. שוט הסוס: ויעש כן.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'avi' as a goad and 'shot ha-sus' as a horse-whip; and 'and he did so' (referring to David's conduct).

rashiTranslations.he: בישעיה כתיב ישעיה לא היה רגיל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the verse cited is in Isaiah - Isaiah was not in the habit of riding in a chariot of horses, and his going barefoot was an actual barefootedness.
Continuing: Isaiah's barefootedness was literal going without shoes, while David's barefootedness was the absence of wearing a sandal.

rashiTranslations.he: סנדל הוא והוא יגלה על יחוף דדוד
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: and 'he shall reveal' bears on the barefootedness of David - that David's case too refers to not wearing a sandal.

rashiTranslations.he: נמי דמנעילת הסנדל הוא: שלא יבוא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: David's barefootedness also means going without a sandal. On 'that your foot not come to barefootedness' -
Rashi glosses 'that your foot not come to barefootedness' (Jer 2): keep from going unshod 'when you go out into exile.'
Rashi begins the next comment ('im' - 'if'), opening the following passage.
